differences.description: The document title changed from “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual: Volume II” to “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II”.
The version and release status changed from “Version 20250508: This document is in Ratified state.” to “Version 20260120: Official Release”.
The contributors list ending punctuation was corrected from “Reinoud Zandijk..” to “Reinoud Zandijk.”.
A new current preface for Version 20260120 was added, stating that the document contains listed module versions “all of which have been ratified”.
The module list for the current release adds “Svrsw60t59b Extension” version 1.0.
The current release module list renames “Smctr” to “Smctr Extension” and “Pointer Masking” to “Pointer-Masking Extensions”.
A new change note was added: “The following changes have been made since version 20250508: Addition of the Svrsw60t59b extension for additional PTE reserved-for-software bits.”
The 20250508 module list now labels Svvptc and Pointer Masking as “Svvptc Extension” and “Pointer-Masking Extensions,” and labels “Smctr” as “Smctr Extension”.

The change bullets since 20241101 now end “Ssqosid Extension for Quality-of-Service Identifiers.” and “Pointer-Masking Extensions.”, adding punctuation and changing the Pointer Masking extension name from singular to plural hyphenated form.
The compatible-change bullet about synchronization requirements was broadened from “changing the PBMTE fields in menvcfg and henvcfg” to “changing the PBMTE and ADUE fields in menvcfg and henvcfg”.
A compatible-change bullet was added: “Defined the format of the memory-mapped msip registers.”
The bullet “Defined optional mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs” was changed to “Defined mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs,” removing “optional”.
The CSR Listing section was reorganized to add subsections for unprivileged, supervisor-level, hypervisor/VS, machine-level, and indirect CSR mappings.
The standalone chapters for Svnapot, Svpbmt, Svinval, Svadu, Svvptc, and Ssqosid were moved under Chapter 12 as Sections 12.7 through 12.13.
A new Section 12.12, “Svrsw60t59b Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59, Version 1.0,” was added.
The Sstc, Sscofpmf, Hypervisor, CFI, Ssdbltrp, Pointer Masking, Instruction Listings, and History chapters were renumbered from Chapters 19–26 to Chapters 13–20.
A new Appendix A, “Historical Rationale for Extensions,” with subsection A.1 for Smepmp rationale, was added.
The reference to “The separate debug specification proposal” was changed to “The separate debug specification,” removing “proposal”.
The description of csr[9:8] was expanded to state that the pattern 10 represents hypervisor CSRs.
The listing introduction changed from “Table 4-Table 8” to “Table 4-Table 7,” because machine-level CSRs are consolidated into a single table and indirect CSR mappings are handled separately.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor indirect CSRs siselect, sireg, sireg2, sireg3, sireg4, sireg5, and sireg6 at addresses 0x150–0x157 were added to the supervisor CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor timer compare CSRs stimecmp and stimecmph at addresses 0x14D and 0x15D were added to the supervisor CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The supervisor resource management configuration CSR srmcfg at address 0x181 was added to the supervisor CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor Control Transfer Records CSRs sctrctl, sctrstatus, and sctrdepth at addresses 0x14E, 0x14F, and 0x15F were added to the supervisor CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: Virtual supervisor indirect CSRs vsiselect, vsireg, vsireg2, vsireg3, vsireg4, vsireg5, and vsireg6 at addresses 0x250–0x257 were added to the hypervisor/VS CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: Virtual supervisor timer compare CSRs vstimecmp and vstimecmph at addresses 0x24D and 0x25D were added to the hypervisor/VS CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: The virtual supervisor Control Transfer Records CSR vsctrctl at address 0x24E was added to the hypervisor/VS CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Machine indirect CSRs miselect, mireg, mireg2, mireg3, mireg4, mireg5, and mireg6 at addresses 0x350–0x357 were added to the machine CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Machine counter configuration CSRs mcyclecfg, minstretcfg, mcyclecfgh, and minstretcfgh at addresses 0x321, 0x322, 0x721, and 0x722 were added to the machine CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The machine Control Transfer Records CSR mctrctl at address 0x34E was added to the machine CSR listing.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: New indirect CSR mapping tables were added for M-mode, S-mode, and VS-mode, including AIA-related mappings such as iprio, eidelivery, eithreshold, eip, and eie.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: The indirect CSR mapping table for S-mode adds delegated counter mappings at siselect values 0x40–0x5F for cycle, instret, hpmcounter, cyclecfg, instretcfg, hpmevent, and corresponding RV32 high-half aliases.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: The indirect CSR mapping tables for S-mode and VS-mode add CTR logical entry mappings at select values 0x200–0x2FF, mapping to ctrsource, ctrtarget, and ctrdata entries with sireg4–sireg6/vsireg4–vsireg6 reading as 0.
The commentary changed “the repo around which development occurs” to “the project around which development occurs.”
The requirement was strengthened from “at least one hart must have a hart ID of zero” to “one hart must have a hart ID of zero.”

differences.section: 3.1.6.2. Double Trap Control in mstatus Register
differences.description: The critical-error state behavior now states that whether performance counters and timers are updated in the critical-error state is UNSPECIFIED.

differences.section: 3.1.6.3. Base ISA Control in mstatus Register
differences.description: The HINT commentary changed “register writeback” to “register write-back.”
The MPRV definition was generalized from modifying the privilege at which “loads and stores execute” to modifying the privilege at which “explicit memory accesses execute.”
The MPRV=0 behavior was generalized from “loads and stores behave as normal” to “explicit memory accesses behave as normal.”
TW=0 behavior was reworded from allowing WFI in “lower privilege modes” to allowing WFI in “modes less privileged than M.”
TW=1 behavior was reworded from allowing WFI always to raise in “less-privileged modes” to “modes less privileged than M.”
The U-mode WFI rule when S-mode is implemented now explicitly says the illegal-instruction exception occurs regardless of TW unless WFI completes within the bounded time limit.
The statement that a future revision might add a feature allowing S-mode to selectively permit WFI in U-mode, active only when TW=0, was removed.

The MSIP description no longer says memory-mapped control registers are used by remote harts or that a hart can write its own MSIP bit through the same memory-mapped control register; it instead generally says the registers are used to provide machine-level interprocessor interrupts.

A new paragraph defines a hart’s memory-mapped msip register as a 32-bit read/write register with bits 31–1 reading zero and bit 0 containing MSIP, and states that changes are reflected in mip.MSIP eventually but not necessarily immediately.

The STIP description was expanded to distinguish behavior when stimecmp is not implemented (STIP writable in mip) from behavior when stimecmp is implemented (STIP read-only in mip and reflecting the supervisor timer interrupt signal from stimecmp).

The TM bit now also controls access to stimecmp and vstimecmp from modes less privileged than M, causing illegal-instruction exceptions when clear and permitting access under the specified S-mode/VS-mode conditions when set.
The text “HPM_n_ bit” was corrected to “HPMn bit.”
The cases in which a nonzero mtval contains the faulting virtual address were expanded to include hardware-error exceptions.
A new paragraph specifies that on a breakpoint exception raised by EBREAK or C.EBREAK, mtval is written with either zero or the virtual address of the instruction.
A new note explains that recording the EBREAK instruction address in mtval is redundant with mepc but is provided for backwards compatibility.
The misaligned load/store case was expanded so mtval contains the virtual address of the faulting portion when the misaligned access causes an access-fault, page-fault, or hardware-error exception.
The variable-length instruction case was expanded so mtval contains the virtual address of the faulting instruction portion for instruction access-fault, page-fault, or hardware-error exceptions.
The mconfigptr alignment formula was clarified to say that if MXLEN is 8×n, then mconfigptr[log2n-1:0] must be zero.

After changing menvcfg.ADUE, software must execute SFENCE.VMA with rs1=x0 and rs2=x0 to synchronize address-translation caches with respect to the altered interpretation of PTE A/D bits, with additional hypervisor synchronization requirements referenced.
The STCE field is now specified directly in the menvcfg section rather than merely saying its definition is furnished by Sstc.

The menvcfg.STCE definition now states that STCE enables stimecmp for S-mode, that it is read-only zero when Sstc is not implemented, and that when zero, non-M-mode stimecmp access traps and STIP behavior reverts as if Sstc were not implemented.

The CBZE field is now specified directly, including that it enables CBO.ZERO in modes less privileged than M and otherwise causes illegal-instruction exceptions; the previous text only said the definition was furnished by Zicboz.

The CBCFE field is now specified directly, including that it enables CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH in modes less privileged than M and otherwise causes illegal-instruction exceptions; the previous text only said the definitions were furnished by Zicbom.
The CBIE field behavior was added, including encodings 00, 01, 10, and 11 and the distinction between flush and invalidate behavior for CBO.INVAL in modes less privileged than M.
The PMM field is now specified directly with legal values for pointer masking instead of merely saying its definition is furnished by Smnpm.
mseccfg changed from “an optional 64-bit read/write register” to a 64-bit register that exists if any extension adds a field to it and is otherwise reserved.
The SSEED and USEED fields are now specified directly as Zkr fields controlling access to the seed CSR from U-mode and S/HS-mode, rather than being merely referenced as furnished by Zkr.
A new Entropy Source Access Control table was added, specifying seed CSR access behavior for M, U, S/HS, and VS/VU modes under SSEED and USEED values.
The RLB, MMWP, and MML fields are now specified directly in the mseccfg section, rather than only being referenced as furnished by Smepmp.
The detailed Smepmp RLB rule-locking bypass definition and its explanatory note were moved into the mseccfg section.
The detailed Smepmp MMWP allowlist-policy definition was moved into the mseccfg section.
The detailed Smepmp MML behavior rules for M-mode-only, S/U-mode-only, and Shared-Region PMP rules were moved into the mseccfg section.
The PMM field is now specified directly with legal values for M-mode pointer masking and an Smmpm implementation note, rather than merely saying its definition is furnished by Smmpm.
The mseccfgh existence rule was clarified from “mseccfgh does not exist when XLEN=64” to “mseccfgh exists when XLEN=32 and mseccfg is implemented; it does not exist when XLEN=64.”
Reset now specifies that the MML, MMWP, and RLB fields of mseccfg are set to 0 unless the platform mandates different reset values.
Reset now specifies that the USEED and SSEED fields of mseccfg must have defined reset values and must not be left undefined after reset.
AMOArithmetic support was clarified from supporting “all RISC-V AMOs” to supporting “all RISC-V AMOs defined by the A extension.”
The Zacas extension’s AMOCASW, AMOCASD, and AMOCASQ PMA support levels were added, defining support for AMOCAS.W, AMOCAS.D, and AMOCAS.Q in addition to AMOArithmetic support.
A note was added explaining that AMOCASW/D/Q require AMOArithmetic-level support because AMOCAS.W/D/Q require arithmetic comparison and swap capability.
The Zabha extension was added to the PMA discussion, specifying that its AMOs require the same level of support as corresponding A-extension or Zacas instructions.
The set of instructions to which the misaligned atomicity granule PMA applies was expanded to include compressed encodings of the listed base and floating-point loads and stores.

PMP permission-fault text now includes cache-block management instructions as load-like operations requiring read permission and cache-block zero instructions as store-like operations requiring write permission.
A new note explains that a TOR PMP cannot represent address 2^(XLEN+2) as the top of a range and gives an RV32 34-bit address-space example where the uppermost word cannot be exposed via TOR.
The statement that if current XLEN is greater than MXLEN, PMP address registers are zero-extended from MXLEN to XLEN for address matching, was removed.
The L-bit description now says R/W/X permissions are “additionally enforced” on M-mode accesses, clarifying that S/U enforcement is already present.
PMP priority and matching rules were rewritten in terms of “memory operations” rather than “accesses,” clarifying behavior when a single instruction decomposes into multiple memory operations.

A new opening paragraph states that on some implementations misaligned loads, stores, and instruction fetches may decompose into multiple memory operations, and that PMP checking is performed independently on each memory operation.

The RV32 description changed from saying the listed stateen registers are 32-bit with corresponding high-half CSRs to saying there are CSR addresses for accessing the upper 32 bits of corresponding machine-level and hypervisor CSRs.
The SRMCFG bit description now refers to the Ssqosid extension without citing Chapter 18.

The introductory description was rewritten to remove explicit SMAP and SMEP terminology and instead describe the two mechanisms generically, while preserving the RISC-V SUM and user-page execution protections.

The section structure changed: “6.1 Introduction,” “6.1.1 Threat model,” “6.2 Proposal,” “6.2.1 Truth table,” “6.2.2 Visual representation,” and “6.4 Rationale” were replaced by “6.1 Threat model,” “6.2 Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules,” and “6.3 Smepmp software discovery.”
The threat model now refers readers to Appendix A.1 for the rationale that guided development of the Smepmp extension.
The threat model text now frames the limitation as “Without the Smepmp extension” rather than “However, there are no such mechanisms available on Machine mode in the current (v1.11) Privileged Spec.”
The sentence comparing attacks on Supervisor mode now says they are “mitigated through the virtual-memory system” instead of “mitigated through SMAP/SMEP.”
The detailed proposal item defining mseccfg as a new CSR at addresses 0x747/0x757 was removed from Chapter 6; mseccfg is now specified in Section 3.1.19.
The detailed proposal item defining mseccfg.RLB was removed from Chapter 6; RLB is now specified in Section 3.1.19.
The detailed proposal item defining mseccfg.MMWP was removed from Chapter 6; MMWP is now specified in Section 3.1.19.
The detailed proposal item defining mseccfg.MML and all subrules for M-mode-only, S/U-mode-only, and Shared-Region rules was removed from Chapter 6; MML is now specified in Section 3.1.19.
The truth table for mseccfg.MML=1 was retained but moved from subsection 6.2.1 to the new Section 6.2 and introduced as a summary of the rules specified in mseccfg.
The discovery text now says “all fields defined in mseccfg as part of this extension” rather than “as part of this proposal.”
The entire Smepmp rationale formerly in Section 6.4 was moved out of Chapter 6 into new Appendix A.1 as historical rationale.
The explicit sentence listing CSR numbers for mcyclecfg, minstretcfg, mcyclecfgh, and minstretcfgh was removed from the Smcntrpmf section; these numbers are now listed in the central CSR listing.
The implicit read affected by IALIGN masking for mnepc was corrected from “the implicit read by the MRET instruction” to “the implicit read by the MNRET instruction.”
The chapter title changed from “Smcdeleg Counter Delegation Extension” to “Smcdeleg/Ssccfg Counter Delegation Extensions,” explicitly including Ssccfg.
Introductory wording was pluralized from “This extension provides” and “This extension also defines” to “These extensions provide” and “These extensions also defines.”
A requirement was added that, for a RISC-V hardware platform, Smcdeleg and Ssccfg must always be implemented in tandem.

The note that hpmeventi and cyclecfg/instretcfg represent subsets of machine-level state was converted into normative text immediately after the indirect HPM mapping table, explicitly stating that MINH is read-only zero through sireg* when Sscofpmf or Smcntrpmf is implemented.
The note identifying Smaia/Ssaia-defined registers now includes mvip as well as mvien and hvien.
ctrtarget now explicitly states that for a not-taken branch it holds the PC of the next sequential instruction following the branch.
The commentary changed “Transfer Type Filtering bits” to “Transfer Type Filter bits” when describing ctrdata.TYPE.
The SRET cross-reference was changed from Section 3.3.2 to “Other Privileged Instructions.”
The cases in which nonzero stval contains the faulting virtual address were expanded to include hardware-error exceptions.
A new paragraph specifies that on a breakpoint exception raised by EBREAK or C.EBREAK, stval is written with either zero or the virtual address of the instruction.
The CBZE field is now specified directly, including U-mode CBO.ZERO enable behavior and dependency on S-mode enablement; the previous text only said the definition was furnished by Zicboz.

The CBCFE field is now specified directly, including U-mode CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH enable behavior and dependency on S-mode enablement; the previous text only said the definitions were furnished by Zicbom.
The CBIE field behavior was added for U-mode CBO.INVAL, including the reserved 10b encoding, dependency on S-mode enablement, and the 01b flush and 11b invalidate encodings.
The PMM field is now specified directly with legal values for U/VU pointer masking, rather than merely saying its definition is furnished by Ssnpm.
The detailed stimecmp CSR specification was incorporated into the Supervisor-Level ISA as new Section 12.1.12 instead of appearing only in the Sstc extension chapter.
The STIP description was expanded to specify behavior when Sstc is not implemented versus when Sstc is implemented and STIP reflects stimecmp, including how writes to stimecmp set and clear sip.STIP.

The explanation of stale translations after PTE modification was broadened: instead of saying either the old or new translation is used unpredictably, it now allows any translation for the address that was valid since the last subsuming SFENCE.VMA.
The SFENCE.VMA discussion now explicitly allows stale translations from different virtual-address widths and global translations since the last subsuming SFENCE.VMA.

A new statement says no SFENCE.VMA is required after enabling or disabling pointer masking because pointer masking applies only to the effective address and does not affect memory-management data structures.

PTE permission-fault text now includes cache-block management instructions as load-like operations requiring read permission and cache-block zero instructions as store-like operations requiring write permission.
The store page-fault sentence now contains “cache-block zero instruction instruction,” adding cache-block zero but also duplicating the word “instruction.”
The A/D-bit update rule changed wording from “tablewalk checks” to “page-table walk checks.”
Step 9 now specifies that the PMA/PMP check is for a store to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE rather than just “a store to pte.”
Step 9 now explicitly says that after setting pte.a and possibly pte.d, the updated pte is stored to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE.
The Svnapot section now states that if the hypervisor extension is also implemented, Svnapot is also supported in G-stage translation.
A new Svrsw60t59b extension was added, reserving PTE bits 60–59 for supervisor software and requiring implementations to ignore them.
The Svrsw60t59b extension specifies that, if the Hypervisor extension is implemented, bits 60–59 of G-stage PTEs are also reserved for supervisor software and ignored by the implementation.
The Svrsw60t59b extension declares a dependency on Sv39.
A rationale note was added explaining that operating systems use reserved PTE bits for metadata and that Sv39/Sv39x4 otherwise require faults if bits 60–59 are nonzero.
The sentence about concurrent workloads was split so that resource allocation and monitoring capabilities are described in a separate paragraph.
The Sstc chapter was shortened to an overview that points to stimecmp, vstimecmp, menvcfg, and henvcfg sections instead of containing the detailed machine/supervisor/hypervisor additions.
The former Section 19.1.1 detailed definition of stimecmp was removed from the Sstc chapter and relocated to Section 12.1.12.
The former Sstc subsections modifying mip/mie, sip/sie, and mcounteren were removed from the Sstc chapter and integrated into the corresponding core CSR sections.

The former Sstc subsections defining vstimecmp, hypervisor interrupt changes, hcounteren changes, and menvcfg/henvcfg STCE support were removed from the Sstc chapter and integrated into the Hypervisor and environment-configuration sections.
The HUPMM field was added to hstatus for RV64, and its behavior for pointer masking of HLV.* and HSV.* instructions in U-mode was specified.
A note was added recommending that the hypervisor copy the guest’s senvcfg.PMM value to hstatus.HUPMM before invoking HLV.* or HSV.* instructions in U-mode.
The STCE field is now specified directly, including that it enables vstimecmp for VS-mode and that access to stimecmp-as-vstimecmp traps when henvcfg.STCE is zero.
The CBZE field is now specified directly for CBO.ZERO execution in VS and VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exception behavior.
The CBCFE field is now specified directly for CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH execution in VS and VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exception behavior.
The CBIE field behavior was added for CBO.INVAL in VS and VU modes, including reserved encoding 10b, HS-qualified checks, virtual-instruction exceptions, and 01b/11b flush/invalidate behavior.
The PMM field is now specified directly with legal values for VS-mode pointer masking, rather than merely saying its definition is furnished by Ssnpm.
The TM bit now also controls VS-mode access to vstimecmp through stimecmp, causing a virtual-instruction exception when clear and permitting access when both hcounteren.TM and mcounteren.TM are set.
A detailed vstimecmp CSR section was added to the Hypervisor chapter, specifying precision, RV32 high-half access, VSTIP generation, interrupt posting, and SEE/SBI compatibility.

differences.section: 15.2.18. Virtual Supervisor Address Translation and Protection (vsatp) Register
differences.description: The note about virtual-machine load/store instructions changed from “misspeculatively executed” not setting VS-stage A bits to “mispredicted” not setting VS-stage A bits.

differences.section: 15.3.1. Hypervisor Virtual-Machine Load and Store Instructions
differences.description: The HLVX.* instructions are now explicitly exempted from pointer masking, with a note explaining that they emulate implicit instruction fetches from guest memory and that pointer masking also does not apply when MXR is set.

The synchronization rule for changing menvcfg was expanded from PBMTE only to PBMTE or ADUE, requiring HFENCE.GVMA synchronization for altered interpretation of G-stage and VS-stage PTE PBMT and A/D bit fields.
The synchronization rule for changing henvcfg was expanded from PBMTE only to PBMTE or ADUE, requiring HFENCE.VVMA synchronization for altered interpretation of VS-stage PTE PBMT and A/D bit fields.
The world-switch note now refers to henvcfg.PBMTE/ADUE needing to be switched, rather than only henvcfg.PBMTE.
A new hypervisor section describes interaction between pointer masking and two-stage address translation for guest physical addresses when VS/VU runs with vsatp.MODE=Bare.

The new pointer-masking interaction section recommends executing HFENCE.GVMA with rs1=x0 when henvcfg.PMM changes to or from values where XLEN-PMLEN is less than the GPA width for Sv57x4 or Sv48x4 cases.

The new pointer-masking interaction section specifies that address-specific HFENCE.GVMA implementations should either ignore the address argument or ignore the top masked GPA bits when comparing for address matches.
In the hypervisor trap cause table, interrupt exception code 13 changed from “Reserved for counter-overflow interrupt” to “Counter-overflow interrupt.”

differences.section: 15.6.3. Transformed Instruction or Pseudoinstruction for mtinst or htinst
differences.description: The figure captions for transformed load and store instructions no longer describe them as “noncompressed,” though the surrounding text still states they are for non-compressed instructions.

The Bare-mode rule was reworded from “shadow stack memory accesses are prohibited, and shadow stack instructions will raise” to the more direct “shadow stack instructions raise” a store/AMO access-fault exception when the effective privilege mode is less than M.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The compressed-instruction list subject to pointer masking no longer includes C.LQSP.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The compressed-instruction list subject to pointer masking no longer includes C.SQSP.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The hardware-write and debug-trigger sentences now qualify pointer masking with “when applicable,” clarifying that masking is only applied when applicable to the address.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The stval example was reworded to say hardware applies pointer masking to any address written into stval if pointer masking is applicable to that address.
The Ssnpm, Smnpm, and Smmpm extension descriptions now refer to the specific CSR sections where their controls are defined, rather than saying details are in Section 24.3.

The standalone “ISA Extensions” section and its subsections Ssnpm, Smnpm, Smmpm, Interaction with SFENCE.VMA, Interaction with Two-Stage Address Translation, and Number of Masked Bits were removed; their content was redistributed to CSR sections, SFENCE.VMA, Hypervisor pointer-masking interaction, and Section 18.2.8.
The standalone pointer-masking statement that no SFENCE.VMA is required after enabling/disabling pointer masking was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter and relocated to the SFENCE.VMA section.
The standalone pointer-masking discussion of two-stage address translation and HFENCE.GVMA requirements was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter and relocated to Hypervisor Section 15.5.4.
The privileged instruction listing now includes a new “Control Transfer Records Management Instructions” category with the SCTRCLR instruction encoding.
The separate Bibliography section at the end of the first document was not retained as a separate top-level section; the bibliography entries appear at the end of Appendix A.1 in the second document.

differences.section: Appendix A: Historical Rationale for Extensions
differences.description: A new appendix was added explaining that extension rationales will be recorded chronologically and that rationales for pre-2025 ratified extensions will be added over time or synthesized from the historical record.
